title: Giving & Asking Directions
situation: A lost person stops you near a market and asks how to get to the nearest metro station.

phrases.context: 'kidhar' = where (direction). More casual and common than 'kahan' for asking directions.

phrases.hindi: seedha jaiye, phir left le lena
phrases.english: Go straight, then take a left
phrases.context: 'seedha' = straight, 'le lena' = take (for directions). 'jaiye' is polite form of 'go'.
phrases.pronunciation: SEE-dha jaa-I-ye, PHIR LEFT le LE-na

phrases.hindi: woh red light ke baad, right side mein hai
phrases.english: It's after the red light, on the right side
phrases.context: 'ke baad' = after. 'Red light' = traffic signal (everyone calls it red light even when it's green)
phrases.pronunciation: woh RED light ke BAAD, RIGHT side mein hai

phrases.hindi: yahan se paanch minute mein pahunch jaoge
phrases.english: You'll reach in 5 minutes from here
phrases.context: 'pahunch jaoge' = you'll reach, gives reassurance about distance/time
phrases.pronunciation: ya-HAAN se PAANCH mi-NUTE mein pa-HUNCH jaa-O-ge

phrases.hindi: uss building ke saamne hai, miss nahi hoga
phrases.english: It's in front of that building, you won't miss it
phrases.context: 'ke saamne' = in front of. 'miss nahi hoga' = can't miss it, classic reassurance
phrases.pronunciation: USS buil-DING ke SAAM-ne hai, MISS na-HI ho-GA

phrases.hindi: idhar nahi, udhar, ulta ja rahe ho
phrases.english: Not this way, that way, you're going the wrong way
phrases.context: 'idhar' = this side, 'udhar' = that side, 'ulta' = opposite/wrong direction
phrases.pronunciation: i-DHAR na-HI, u-DHAR, UL-ta ja ra-HE ho

phrases.hindi: Big Bazaar pata hai? uske bilkul peeche hai
phrases.english: Do you know Big Bazaar? It's right behind it
phrases.context: Using landmark-based directions, 'pata hai?' checks if they know the reference point
phrases.pronunciation: big ba-ZAAR pa-TA hai? us-KE bil-KUL PEE-che hai

phrases.hindi: arey, bahut door hai yahan se, auto le lo
phrases.english: Oh, it's very far from here, take an auto
phrases.context: When the place is too far to walk, honest redirection is appreciated
phrases.pronunciation: a-REY, ba-HUT DOOR hai ya-HAAN se, AU-to le LO

phrases.hindi: kisi se bhi pooch lena, sab ko pata hai
phrases.context: Common Indian ending to directions, crowdsource the remaining navigation
phrases.pronunciation: ki-SI se bhi POOCH le-NA, SAB ko pa-TA hai

phrases.hindi: Google Maps lagao na, main bhi confuse ho jaunga
phrases.english: Use Google Maps, even I'll get confused
phrases.context: Honest admission when you're not 100% sure, offer the modern solution
phrases.pronunciation: GOO-gle MAPS la-GAO na, MAIN bhi con-FUSE ho jaa-UN-ga

grammar_notes: Hindi uses postpositions (come AFTER the noun): 'ghar mein' = in the house, 'station ke paas' = near the station, 'market ke saamne' = in front of the market
'ke' connects nouns to postpositions: 'market KE saamne' = in front of market. The noun before 'ke' changes form: 'ghar' stays same, but 'mera' becomes 'mere'.
'le lena' (take), 'mud jaana' (turn), 'pahunch jaana' (arrive), compound verbs for directions add a sense of completion
'jaiye' (go-polite) vs 'jao' (go-friendly) vs 'ja' (go-very casual), use 'jaiye' with strangers asking directions
'pata hai?' = do you know?, use this before giving a landmark reference to check if they'll understand
culture_notes: Indians give directions using landmarks, not street names: 'gol chakkar ke baad' (after the roundabout), 'mandir ke saamne' (in front of the temple)
Everyone will help with directions, sometimes even if they don't know the way. They'll guess confidently.
'Thoda aage' (a little ahead) can mean anywhere from 100m to 2km, always ask 'kitna dur?' (how far?)
If someone points with their chin/head instead of hand, that's normal, not rude, just efficient

skill_breakdown.explanation: Unlike English prepositions (in, on, at) that come BEFORE nouns, Hindi postpositions come AFTER. Key ones: mein (in/inside), pe/par (on/at), ke paas (near), ke saamne (in front of), ke peeche (behind), ke upar (above), ke neeche (below), ke beech mein (in between).

more_examples.hindi: mere ghar ke paas ek park hai
more_examples.english: there's a park near my house

more_examples.hindi: dono buildings ke beech mein gali hai
more_examples.english: there's a lane between both buildings

skill_breakdown.explanation: seedha (straight), left/right (same in Hindi), aage (ahead), peeche (behind), yahan (here), wahan (there), idhar (this side), udhar (that side), andar (inside), bahar (outside), upar (up), neeche (down), saamne (in front), door (far), paas (near).

more_examples.english: look in front, there it is

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Pehle' (first), 'phir' (then), 'uske baad' (after that), 'last mein' (finally). Chain these to give step-by-step instructions. Each step: direction + landmark. Keep sentences short and visual.

more_examples.hindi: pehle seedha jao, phir signal pe left
more_examples.english: first go straight, then left at the signal

more_examples.hindi: uske baad ek chemist dikhega, wahan se right
more_examples.english: after that you'll see a chemist, turn right from there

more_examples.hindi: phir thoda aur aage, left side pe hai
more_examples.english: then a bit further ahead, it's on the left side

more_examples.hindi: agar Domino's dikh jaye toh zyada aage nikal gaye
more_examples.english: if you see Domino's you've gone too far

practice_prompt: You are a hurried, slightly anxious stranger in Lajpat Nagar Central Market trying to find the metro station entry gate, train to catch in 20 minutes. The user is a local you've stopped on the footpath. Use 'aap' throughout, polite but rushed, and speak only Hindi. Open with exactly: 'excuse me ji, aap mujhe bata sakte hain, Lajpat Nagar metro station kahan hai?' Then run a 6-8 turn conversation: after their first directions, get genuinely confused at ONE point (an unfamiliar landmark, or which side to turn after the signal) and ask a clarifier like 'matlab Haldiram ke saamne se left, ya uske baad?'. If they suggest Google Maps, say your signal is weak, 'main mobile nahi chala raha abhi'. Use postpositions ('ke saamne', 'ke baad'), sequence markers ('phir', 'uske baad'), and distance phrases ('paanch minute', 'kitna dur') naturally. When the user errs (wrong postposition, missing oblique form, English-style sequencing), flag it inline as [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]] and continue. End with 'thik hai, bahut shukriya ji, chalta hoon' and a wave.
